title: Building a physics-aware AI ecosystem for solid-state hydrogen storage materials
authors: Seong-Hoon Jang, Yiwen Yao, Chuanyu Liu, Linda Zhang, Di Zhang, Xue Jia, Hung Ba Tran, Eric Jianfeng Cheng, Ryuhei Sato, Yusuke Ohashi, Toyoto Sato, Yusuke Hashimoto, Mark Allendorf, Nongnuch Artrith, Marcello Baricco, Andreas Borgschulte, Darren P. Broom, Ang Cao, Benjamin W. J. Chen, Lixin Chen, Ping Chen, Eun Seon Cho, Stefano Deledda, Zhao Ding, Martin Dornheim, Michael Felderhoff, Yaroslav Filinchuk, George E. Froudakis, Mingxia Gao, Thomas Gennett, Zaiping Guo, Ikutaro Hamada, Jason Hattrick-Simpers, Bjørn C. Hauback, Michael Hirscher, Torben R. Jensen, Baohua Jia, Hyoung Seop Kim, Takahiro Kondo, Kentaro Kutsukake, Xiao-Yan Li, Tongliang Liu, Piao Ma, Jianfeng Mao, Rana Mohtadi, Hyunchul Oh, Mark Paskevicius, Chris J. Pickard, Astrid Pundt, Long Qi, Anibal Ramirez-Cuesta, Hiroyuki Saitoh, Kaihang Shi, Aloysius Soon, Chenghua Sun, Chris Wolverton, Hiroshi Yabu, Weijie Yang, Zhenpeng Yao, Xuebin Yu, Jianxin Zou, Shouyi Hu, Panpan Zhou, Xi Lin, Zhigang Hu, Zhenhao Zhou, Pengfei Ou, Jiayu Peng, Shin-ichi Orimo, Hao Li
abstract: Hydrogen storage remains a central bottleneck for scalable hydrogen energy systems due to the multiscale and coupled nature of the thermodynamics, kinetics, and microstructural evolution of hydrogen storage materials (HSMs). Although artificial intelligence (AI) has accelerated materials discovery, current approaches remain constrained by fragmented data, limited physical consistency, and weak integration with experimental validation. Here, we propose a unified framework that integrates coherent data infrastructure, physics-grounded modeling, and AI-driven inverse design within a closed-loop discovery paradigm. By embedding physical constraints and experimental feedback, this approach enables adaptive, physically consistent optimization, thereby establishing a pathway toward autonomous, digital-twin-enabled discovery of HSMs.
summary: This paper proposes a new paradigm for materials discovery that moves beyond simple data-driven AI by embedding physical laws and experimental feedback into a closed-loop ecosystem. By integrating MLIPs, generative models, and LLMs, the authors aim to accelerate the development of efficient solid-state hydrogen storage materials.
main_problem: The discovery of high-performance solid-state hydrogen storage materials is hindered by fragmented data, a lack of physical consistency in AI models, and the difficulty of capturing multiscale, path-dependent dynamics like kinetics and degradation.
main_result: The authors propose a unified, physics-aware AI ecosystem that integrates coherent data infrastructure, physics-grounded modeling (such as MLIPs), and a closed-loop discovery paradigm using generative models and LLM agents.
method: The framework combines Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials (MLIPs) for multiscale dynamics, Generative Models (GMs) for structural design, and Large Language Models (LLMs) for knowledge mining and enforcing physical constraints.
model_or_system: The physical systems include solid-state hydrogen storage materials (HSMs) such as metal hydrides, nanoporous frameworks (MOFs, COFs), and high-entropy alloys, spanning scales from atomistic defects to macroscopic performance.
key_observables: Hydrogen uptake (gravimetric/volumetric), adsorption/desorption enthalpy and entropy, diffusion coefficients, pressure-composition isotherms, and phase transformation kinetics.
important_parameters: Thermodynamic windows (delta H), activation energy (Ea), temperature, pressure, and the spatiotemporal scales from electronic structure to mesoscale morphology.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes that integrating physical constraints into AI architectures can overcome the limitations of purely data-driven models and that MLIPs can bridge the gap between DFT accuracy and experimental scales.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the disconnect between computational and experimental data and the proposed unified database; Figure 2 illustrates MLIP-driven multiscale modeling of hydrogen diffusion and microvoid nucleation; Figure 3 depicts the dual-stream GM-LLM architecture for inverse design; Figure 4 shows the closed-loop discovery workflow.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the bottlenecks in hydrogen storage research, proposes a unified physics-aware AI ecosystem, details the integration of data infrastructure, modeling (MLIPs), and generative AI, and concludes with a vision for autonomous, agent-driven discovery.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
